title: Checkpoint 3, Entropy & Gibbs Free Energy
subtitle: Boss-fight synthesis of Module 4 Lessons 11‚Äď13
subject: Chemistry
syllabusDotPoints: Synthesise IQ3, entropy sign prediction, őĒS¬į calculation, and Gibbs free energy spontaneity
lessonIntent: Students can predict őĒS sign, calculate őĒS¬į from a table, then combine with őĒH¬į to find őĒG¬į and decide spontaneity.
examSkill: Move from qualitative őĒS prediction ‚Üí quantitative őĒS¬į ‚Üí őĒG¬į = őĒH¬į ‚ąí TőĒS¬į, including the kJ/J unit conversion.
productionNotes: Final boss-fight covering M4 L11-L13. Three walkthroughs: predict őĒS sign, calculate őĒS¬į from table, calculate őĒG¬į + decide spontaneity + T_cross. Pitfalls: always-positive-őĒS, element-S¬į-zero, spontaneous-means-fast. Ends Module 4.

scenes.caption: Checkpoint 3, IQ3: Entropy & Gibbs. Year 11 Chemistry, Module 4.

voiceover.text: Checkpoint three. Entropy and Gibbs Free Energy. Year eleven Chemistry, Module four.

scenes.heading: Will the reaction actually go? Three steps to the answer.
scenes.body: IQ3 is the climax of the module. To decide whether a reaction is spontaneous, you walk a ladder: predict őĒS, calculate őĒS¬į, then combine with őĒH¬į via Gibbs. One process, three skills.

bullets.text: Step 1: predict őĒS sign from gas moles (L11).
Step 2: calculate őĒS¬į from a table (L12).
Step 3: őĒG¬į = őĒH¬į ‚ąí TőĒS¬į ‚Üí sign decides (L13).

scenes.callout: Three steps from disorder prediction to a spontaneity answer.
scenes.caption: őĒS sign ‚Üí őĒS¬į value ‚Üí őĒG¬į; the IQ3 ladder.

voiceover.text: IQ3 is the climax of the module. To decide whether a reaction is spontaneous, you climb a ladder of three skills. First, predict the sign of delta S by counting gas moles ‚ÄĒ that's qualitative. Second, calculate delta S zero from a standard entropy table ‚ÄĒ that's quantitative. Third, combine with delta H zero using the Gibbs equation ‚ÄĒ delta G zero equals delta H zero minus T delta S zero. The sign of delta G zero is your final answer. Negative means thermodynamically spontaneous.

scenes.heading: The IQ3 decision tree
scenes.body: Read the question and match it to the right skill.

bullets.text: Asked for őĒS sign? ‚Üí Count gas moles (L11).
Given a table of S¬į values? ‚Üí ő£ products ‚ąí ő£ reactants (L12).
Asked for spontaneity at given T? ‚Üí őĒG¬į = őĒH¬į ‚ąí TőĒS¬į (L13).
Asked for crossover T? ‚Üí T = őĒH¬į/őĒS¬į (L13).

scenes.callout: Sign ‚Üí S¬į value ‚Üí Gibbs. Crossover T = őĒH¬į/őĒS¬į.
scenes.caption: Decision tree for IQ3 skills.

voiceover.text: The IQ3 decision tree. Think of it like climbing a three-rung ladder ‚ÄĒ predict, calculate, then combine. But unlike a physical ladder, you don't always need all three rungs; the question tells you where to start. Asked for the sign of delta S ‚ÄĒ count gas moles from Lesson eleven. Given a table of standard entropies ‚ÄĒ apply products minus reactants from Lesson twelve. Asked for spontaneity at a given temperature ‚ÄĒ use Gibbs from Lesson thirteen, delta G equals delta H minus T delta S. Asked for the crossover temperature ‚ÄĒ set delta G to zero, so T equals delta H over delta S.

scenes.heading: Walkthrough 1, predict őĒS sign
scenes.question: Predict the sign of őĒS for: (a) 2NO(g) + O‚āā(g) ‚Üí 2NO‚āā(g); (b) CaCO‚āÉ(s) ‚Üí CaO(s) + CO‚āā(g); (c) H‚āāO(l) ‚Üí H‚āāO(s).
scenes.coachNote: Count gas moles on each side. State changes matter too.
steps: Known: three equations with different gas-mole changes.
Find: sign of őĒS for each.
(a) Reactant gas = 3 mol; product gas = 2 mol; őĒn = ‚ąí1 ‚Üí őĒS negative.
(b) Reactant gas = 0; product gas = 1; őĒn = +1 ‚Üí őĒS positive.
Formula: more disorder = more arrangements (gas > liquid > solid).
Substitute: count őĒn(gas) for each = (a) 2 ‚ąí 3 = -1; (b) 1 ‚ąí 0 = 1; (c) state change l ‚Üí s.
(c) Liquid ‚Üí solid ‚Üí őĒS strongly negative (freezing).
Answer = 3 signs predicted: (a) negative; (b) positive; (c) negative (freezing is ordering).
scenes.caption: Predictions: (a) ‚ąí (b) + (c) ‚ąí . Gas moles + state change.

voiceover.text: Walkthrough one ‚ÄĒ predict three signs. Part a: two NO plus O two giving two NO two ‚ÄĒ three moles of gas become two. Delta n is minus one, so delta S negative. Part b: calcium carbonate decomposing ‚ÄĒ zero gas moles becomes one. Delta n plus one, delta S positive. Part c: liquid water freezing to solid ice ‚ÄĒ that's an ordering process, delta S strongly negative.

scenes.heading: Walkthrough 2, calculate őĒS¬į
scenes.question: Calculate őĒS¬į for 2H‚āā(g) + O‚āā(g) ‚Üí 2H‚āāO(l). Use S¬į[H‚āā(g)] = 130.7, S¬į[O‚āā(g)] = 205.2, S¬į[H‚āāO(l)] = 69.9 J K‚ĀĽ¬Ļ mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ.
scenes.coachNote: Sum products ‚ąí sum reactants, scaled by coefficients. Verify sign against őĒn(gas).
steps: Known: S¬į values; coefficients in the equation.
Find: őĒS¬į in J K‚ĀĽ¬Ļ mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ.
ő£ S¬į(products) = 2 √ó 69.9 = 139.8 J K‚ĀĽ¬Ļ mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ.
Formula: őĒS¬į = ő£ S¬į(products) ‚ąí ő£ S¬į(reactants).
Substitute: őĒS¬į = 139.8 ‚ąí 466.6.
Check: 3 mol gas ‚Üí 0 mol gas (liquid), strongly negative, confirmed.
Answer: őĒS¬į = 326.8 J K‚ĀĽ¬Ļ mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ decrease (i.e. ‚ąí326.8 J K‚ĀĽ¬Ļ mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ).
scenes.caption: őĒS¬į = 139.8 ‚ąí 466.6 = ‚ąí326.8 J K‚ĀĽ¬Ļ mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ.

voiceover.text: Walkthrough two ‚ÄĒ calculate delta S zero for hydrogen combustion to liquid water. Sum products: two times sixty-nine-point-nine, one-thirty-nine-point-eight. Sum reactants: two times one-thirty-point-seven plus one times two-zero-five-point-two, total four-sixty-six-point-six. Delta S zero is one-thirty-nine-point-eight minus four-sixty-six-point-six ‚ÄĒ minus three hundred and twenty-six-point-eight joules per kelvin per mole. Three gas moles become two liquid moles ‚ÄĒ strongly negative confirms the qualitative prediction.

scenes.heading: Walkthrough 3, Gibbs spontaneity + crossover T
scenes.question: For N‚āā(g) + 3H‚āā(g) ‚Üí 2NH‚āÉ(g), őĒH¬į = ‚ąí92.4 kJ mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ and őĒS¬į = ‚ąí198.9 J K‚ĀĽ¬Ļ mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ. (a) őĒG¬į at 25 ¬įC? (b) Crossover T where őĒG¬į = 0?
scenes.coachNote: Convert őĒS¬į to kJ. Convert T to K. Watch the double negative.
steps: Known: őĒH¬į = ‚ąí92.4 kJ mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ, őĒS¬į = ‚ąí198.9 J K‚ĀĽ¬Ļ mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ.
Find: őĒG¬į at 25 ¬įC and crossover T.
Convert: T = 298 K, őĒS¬į = ‚ąí0.1989 kJ K‚ĀĽ¬Ļ mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ.
Formula: őĒG¬į = őĒH¬į ‚ąí TőĒS¬į.
őĒG¬į(298 K) = ‚ąí33.1 kJ mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ ‚Üí spontaneous at 25 ¬įC.
Formula: set őĒG¬į = 0 ‚Üí T_cross = őĒH¬į/őĒS¬į.
(b) Substitute: T_cross = (‚ąí92.4)/(‚ąí0.1989).
Answer: (a) őĒG¬į = 33.1 kJ mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ released; (b) T_cross = 465 K (192 ¬įC).
scenes.caption: őĒG¬į(298 K) = ‚ąí33.1 kJ mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ; T_cross ‚Čą 465 K.

voiceover.text: Walkthrough three ‚ÄĒ Haber Gibbs. Delta H is minus ninety-two-point-four, delta S is minus one-ninety-eight-point-nine joules per kelvin per mole. Convert: temperature is two-ninety-eight kelvin, delta S in kilojoules is minus zero-point-one-nine-eight-nine. Substitute: delta G equals minus ninety-two-point-four minus two-ninety-eight times minus zero-point-one-nine-eight-nine. Watch the double negative ‚ÄĒ that becomes minus ninety-two-point-four plus fifty-nine-point-two-seven, giving minus thirty-three-point-one kilojoules per mole. Negative, so spontaneous at room temperature. Crossover temperature: set delta G to zero ‚ÄĒ T equals delta H over delta S, both negatives cancel, four hundred and sixty-five kelvin, about one-ninety-two Celsius.

scenes.heading: Three classic IQ3 pitfalls
scenes.body: Students often get these wrong: (1) thinking őĒS is always positive because the Second Law says so, wrong, that's the universe; the system alone can decrease. (2) Setting S¬į of elements to zero (confusing it with őĒH¬įf), wrong, S¬į of every substance is positive. (3) Treating a negative őĒG as a guarantee of fast reaction, wrong, that's thermodynamics, not kinetics. The correct rules: system őĒS can be negative, elements have nonzero S¬į, spontaneous ‚Č† fast.
scenes.callout: System őĒS can be negative ¬∑ elements S¬į ‚Č† 0 ¬∑ spontaneous ‚Č† fast.
scenes.caption: Three IQ3 traps: always-positive-őĒS, element-S¬į-zero, spontaneous-means-fast.

voiceover.text: Three classic IQ3 traps. One: students think delta S of the system is always positive because the Second Law says so. Wrong instinct. The correct rule: the Second Law applies to the universe; the system alone can decrease in entropy, as long as the surroundings compensate. Two: students set standard entropy of elements to zero, confusing it with delta H of formation. Wrong instinct. The correct rule: every substance has positive standard entropy ‚ÄĒ elements included. Three: students treat a negative delta G as a guarantee the reaction will go quickly. Wrong instinct. The correct rule: thermodynamics says it can go; kinetics says how fast. They're independent.

scenes.heading: Boss-fight quick check
scenes.question: For C‚āāH‚āĄ(g) + H‚āā(g) ‚Üí C‚āāH‚āÜ(g), őĒH¬į = ‚ąí137 kJ mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ and őĒS¬į = ‚ąí120.7 J K‚ĀĽ¬Ļ mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ. (a) Predict őĒS sign qualitatively. (b) Calculate őĒG¬į at 25 ¬įC. (c) Find T_cross.
scenes.pausePrompt: Pause: predict from gas moles, then Gibbs at 298 K, then T_cross = őĒH¬į/őĒS¬į.
answerSteps: (a) 2 gas moles ‚Üí 1 gas mole ‚Üí őĒS negative (matches the given ‚ąí120.7).
Convert: T = 298 K, őĒS¬į = ‚ąí0.1207 kJ K‚ĀĽ¬Ļ mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ.
Answer: (a) őĒS negative; (b) őĒG¬į = 101 kJ mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ released (spontaneous); (c) T_cross ‚Čą 1135 K.
scenes.caption: őĒS ‚ąí; őĒG¬į(298)=‚ąí101 kJ mol‚ĀĽ¬Ļ (spontaneous); T_cross ‚Čą 1135 K.

voiceover.text: Boss-fight quick check. Hydrogenation of ethene to ethane. Delta H minus one-thirty-seven, delta S minus one-twenty-point-seven joules per kelvin per mole. Pause and try. Part a: two gas moles become one, so delta S negative ‚ÄĒ matches the given value. Part b: convert delta S to kilojoules, minus zero-point-one-two-zero-seven. Substitute at two-ninety-eight kelvin: minus one-thirty-seven minus two-ninety-eight times minus zero-point-one-two-zero-seven, which is minus one-thirty-seven plus thirty-five-point-nine-seven ‚ÄĒ minus one-hundred-and-one kilojoules per mole. Spontaneous. Part c: crossover T equals delta H over delta S ‚ÄĒ both negatives cancel ‚ÄĒ eleven hundred and thirty-five kelvin, about eight hundred and sixty-two degrees Celsius.

scenes.heading: IQ3 closing summary
scenes.finalPrompt: Always check: spontaneous means thermodynamically allowed, never automatically fast.
points: Predict őĒS sign qualitatively from gas moles (L11).
Calculate őĒS¬į = ő£ S¬į(products) ‚ąí ő£ S¬į(reactants) (L12); elements ‚Č† 0.
Convert őĒS¬į from J to kJ before Gibbs.
őĒG¬į = őĒH¬į ‚ąí TőĒS¬į decides spontaneity; T in kelvin.
scenes.caption: Three skills, one Gibbs equation, one decision rule.

voiceover.text: IQ3 closing summary ‚ÄĒ and the end of Module four. Five takeaways. One: predict delta S sign qualitatively from gas moles. Two: calculate delta S zero as sum of products minus reactants ‚ÄĒ and remember, standard entropy of elements is not zero. Three: convert delta S zero from joules to kilojoules before using it in Gibbs. Four: delta G zero equals delta H zero minus T delta S zero decides spontaneity, with T in kelvin. Five: the crossover temperature is delta H over delta S ‚ÄĒ and remember, spontaneous never automatically means fast. Decision rule: spontaneous is thermodynamically allowed ‚ÄĒ kinetics is a separate question.
